title: [2/n] Migrate per_token_group_quant to torch stable ABI
body:
[See https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/36058/changes/c3bd0530831a06e0f5298c34e18e66e3e58a5644 to view the changes in this PR](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/36058/changes/c3bd0530831a06e0f5298c34e18e66e3e58a5644) (otherwise github renders moved files with more than 50% changed lines as deleted and then added) ## Purpose https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/26946 Stacked on https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/31509 ## Test Plan pytest tests/kernels/quantization/test_per_token_group_quant.py ## Test Result ---
Essential Elements of an Effective PR Description Checklist - [ ] The purpose of the PR, such as "Fix some issue (link existing issues this PR will resolve)". - [ ] The test plan, such as providing test command. - [ ] The test results, such as pasting the results comparison before and after, or e2e results - [ ] (Optional) The necessary documentation update, such as updating `supported_models.md` and `examples` for a new model. - [ ] (Optional) Release notes update. If your change is user facing, please update the release notes draft in the [Google Doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1YyVqrgX4gHTtrstbq8oWUImOyPCKSGnJ7xtTpmXzlRs/edit?tab=t.0).
author: mikaylagawarecki

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request aims to migrate the `per_token_group_quant` and `permute_cols` operations to the PyTorch stable ABI, involving moving source files to a new `csrc/stable` directory, creating a `_C_stable_libtorch` extension, and updating code for stable ABI compatibility. However, critical and high-severity memory safety issues were identified in the migrated code. Specifically, the `per_token_group_quant_8bit_packed` function contains a logic error in its shape validation, leading to a deterministic out-of-bounds write on the GPU when the number of tokens is not a multiple of 4. Other kernels also lack sufficient validation for tensor sizes and index values, which could be exploited for memory corruption or information leakage. These security issues must be addressed to ensure the stability and security of the inference engine. Additionally, consider simplifying the CMake configuration by removing redundant conditional checks for improved maintainability.
Looks good overall as long as tests pass. Left a round of minor comments, most important is just that device_index=-1 is correct for when it's not specified.

commits.message:
Move per_token_group_quant files from csrc to csrc/libtorch_stable

Signed-off-by: Mikayla Gawarecki <mikaylagawarecki@gmail.com>

[2/n] Migrate per_token_group_quant to torch stable ABI

Move FP8/INT8 quantization kernels from _C to _C_stable_libtorch:
- quantization/w8a8/fp8/per_token_group_quant.cu (per-token-group FP8 quant)
- quantization/w8a8/int8/per_token_group_quant.cu (per-token-group INT8 quant)

Signed-off-by: Mikayla Gawarecki <mikaylagawarecki@gmail.com>

title: [compile] Add some more startup tests for top models
body:
This PR adds some more counter-based torch.compile startup time tests for top models. The goal is for us to continuously refresh these tests with the top models. I chose an older version of GLM because vLLM's HF range does not fully support the newer GLM models.

The way the vLLM-torch.compile integration works right now is that we will do graph splitting to create subgraphs and then only produce compiled artifacts for the unique subgraphs. For these models, we check that the number of unique subgraphs is much less than the total number of layers, otherwise something has gone wrong.

These tests make smaller versions of the models with dummy weights to keep the compile times low while still being able to test the unique subgraph behavior.

There are two followups after this PR:
- early-exit the inductor compilation, will make these tests run 2x faster
- figure out what is going on with deepseek v3.2 and kimi k2.5 warm start time.
author: zou3519

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces parametrized tests for model startup, verifying the caching behavior of compilation artifacts during cold and warm starts for several different models. The review suggests refactoring the artifact checking logic into a shared helper function to reduce code duplication and improve maintainability.

commits.message:
[compile] Add some more startup tests for top models

This PR adds some more counter-based torch.compile startup time tests
for top models. The goal is for us to continuously refresh these tests
with the top models. I chose an older version of GLM because vLLM's HF
range does not fully support the newer GLM models.

The way the vLLM-torch.compile integration works right now is that we
will do graph splitting to create subgraphs and then only produce
compiled artifacts for the unique subgraphs. For these models, we check
that the number of unique subgraphs is much less than the total number
of layers, otherwise something has gone wrong.

These tests make smaller versions of the models with dummy weights to
keep the compile times low while still being able to test the unique
subgraph behavior.

There are two followups after this PR:
- early-exit the inductor compilation, will make these tests run 2x
  faster
- figure out what is going on with deepseek v3.2 and kimi k2.5 warm
  start time.

Signed-off-by: Richard Zou <zou3519@gmail.com>

title: [Kernel] Optimize SM120 CUTLASS blockwise FP8 GEMM
body:
## Purpose
The SM120 blockwise FP8 GEMM dispatch used a single `Shape<_128, _128, _128>` / `KernelScheduleAuto` configuration for all problem sizes , leaving significant performance on the table during decode (small-M) workloads.

To address this, this PR splits the dispatch logic based on the $M$ dimension:
  - **Small/medium M** (`M<=256`): `Shape<_64,_128,_128>` + `KernelTmaWarpSpecializedPingpong` — reduces tile overhead, improves SM utilization during decode
  - **Large M** (prefill): `Shape<_128,_128,_128>` + `KernelScheduleAuto` — unchanged high-throughput path

ref: https://github.com/sgl-project/sglang/pull/20887
## Test Plan

## Test Result
Profile the GEMM kernel across typical LLM shapes(Qwen3.5-27B-TP1):

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request optimizes the SM120 blockwise FP8 GEMM by dispatching different kernels based on the problem size M. This is a solid approach that yields significant performance improvements for smaller M dimensions, as shown in the test results. The implementation is clean and directly reflects the described optimization strategy. I have one suggestion to improve maintainability by replacing a magic number with a named constant.

title: [Feature] EPLB Support for GPU Model Runner v2
body:
## Purpose

EPLB Support for GPU Model Runner v2

## Test

### Unit test

Added in the `tests/v1/worker/test_gpu_model_runner_v2_eplb.py`

### Acc

`export MODEL="Qwen/Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507-FP8"`

`vllm serve $MODEL -tp 2 -dp 2  --port 9256 --enable-expert-parallel --profiler-config.profiler=torch --profiler-config.torch_profiler_dir=/home/yewentao256/profile_vllm --enable-eplb --eplb-config '{"window_size":4,"step_interval":4,"log_balancedness":true}'`

`lm_eval --model local-completions --model_args "base_url=http://127.0.0.1:9256/v1/completions,model=$MODEL,num_concurrent=1024" --tasks gsm8k`

author: yewentao256

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request adds support for Expert Parallelism Load Balancing (EPLB) to the v2 GPU model runner. The changes are well-structured, introducing an `EPLBGPUModelRunnerMixin` to encapsulate EPLB-related logic and integrating it into the `GPUModelRunner`. New tests are added to verify the functionality. My review focuses on ensuring robustness. I've identified a potential crash scenario when EPLB is enabled with a non-MoE main model but a MoE speculator, and provided a suggestion to handle this case gracefully.
Thanks for the PR! I've made a small change: Added a decorator pattern to invoke `self.eplb.step()` for the certain methods.

title: [Feature] Support per-draft-model MoE backend via `--speculative-config`
body:
## Summary

Adds a `moe_backend` field to `SpeculativeConfig`, allowing users to select a different MoE kernel backend for the draft/proposer model than the one used by the target (generator) model. The override is applied through a new `create_vllm_config_for_spec_decode()` base config builder, with `create_vllm_config_for_draft_model()` extending it for standalone draft models.

## Motivation

In speculative decoding setups where the generator is quantized (e.g. FP8/NvFP4) but the drafter is unquantized (or vice-versa), the optimal MoE backend for each model can differ. Today vLLM applies a single `--moe-backend` to both, forcing users to compromise or rely on `"auto"` heuristics that may not be ideal for the drafter. This change lets users explicitly control the drafter's MoE backend independently.

## Design

- **`create_vllm_config_for_spec_decode()`** (new, in `utils.py`): base config builder that applies kernel-level overrides (currently `moe_backend`). Used by all proposers.
- **`create_vllm_config_for_draft_model()`** (refactored): extends the above with `quant_config`, `parallel_config`, and `model_config` overrides specific to standalone draft models.
- **`SpecDecodeBaseProposer._create_draft_vllm_config()`** (new method): calls `create_vllm_config_for_spec_decode`. Overridden by `DraftModelProposer` to call `create_vllm_config_for_draft_model` instead.
- **Medusa**: calls `create_vllm_config_for_spec_decode` directly (does not extend `SpecDecodeBaseProposer`).

### Usage

```bash
vllm serve <target_model> \
  --moe-backend flashinfer_trtllm \
  --speculative-config '{"method":"mtp","num_speculative_tokens":1,"moe_backend":"triton"}'
```

## Test plan

[x] Parametrized `test_draft_moe_backend` covering `draft_model`, `eagle`, and `nemotron_mtp` methods × 3 backend scenarios (override, inherit, default auto).
[x] `test_create_vllm_config_for_spec_decode_noop_without_spec_config` for the no-speculative-config edge case.
[x] Existing speculative decoding tests pass unchanged.
[x] E2E test with small eagle model on H100
author: askliar

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces a `moe_backend` option within `SpeculativeConfig` to enable distinct MoE kernel backends for draft models in speculative decoding. The implementation correctly integrates this new configuration, applying it during the creation of the draft model's configuration. The changes are logical and well-implemented. After a thorough review, I found no issues of `high` or `critical` severity.
Does not seem to apply to all LLM speculators, only draft models. Why?
LGTM
Please use `vllm.config.utils.replace` instead of `dataclasses.replace`. `dataclasses.replace` is not guaranteed to work on Pydantic dataclasses (which all our config classes are).
Thanks for making the change!

commits.message:
[Feature] Add MoE backend configuration for draft models in SpeculativeConfig

- Introduced `moe_backend` attribute to `SpeculativeConfig` to specify the MoE backend for draft models.
- Updated `create_vllm_config_for_draft_model` to handle the new `moe_backend` setting, ensuring compatibility between drafter and generator configurations.

Signed-off-by: Andrii Skliar <askliar@nvidia.com>

[Feature] Implement apply_draft_moe_backend utility and enhance draft model tests

- Added `apply_draft_moe_backend` function to override `moe_backend` in `VllmConfig` based on `speculative_config`.
- Updated `eagle.py` and `medusa.py` to utilize the new utility for model configuration.
- Introduced parameterized tests in `test_spec_decode.py` to validate the behavior of draft model configurations with various `moe_backend` scenarios.

Signed-off-by: [Andrii Skliar] <askliar@nvidia.com>
Signed-off-by: Andrii Skliar <askliar@nvidia.com>

fix formatting

Signed-off-by: Andrii Skliar <askliar@nvidia.com>

[Test] Enhance draft model tests with parameterization and additional assertions

title: [Docs] Add guide for editing agent instruction files
body:
## Purpose

This establishes best practices for maintaining AGENTS.md and domain guides: token budget limits, when NOT to add content, where content belongs, what makes a good domain guide, anti-patterns to avoid, and a change checklist.

The goal is to avoid AGENTS.md bloat while also giving a clear path to referencing additional domain-specific information from AGENTS.md as we improve the agentic developer experience of vLLM.

## Test Plan

After making these changes, I ask Claude Code to help me make a few edits to AGENTS.md and observe its response to ensure it's guiding users to best practices.

## Test Result


### Test 1 - copy large swaths of existing content into AGENTS.md

```
❯ Copy all of docs/contributing/README.md into our AGENTS.md

● Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

● Before modifying AGENTS.md, I need to check the editing guide it references, since the domain-specific guides section says I must read it first.

● Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

● I need to refuse this change. Here's why:

  The editing guide at docs/contributing/editing-agent-instructions.md explicitly prohibits this:

  1. Token budget: AGENTS.md must stay under 200 lines. It's currently 126 lines. Copying the 304-line README.md into it would blow it to ~430 lines — 2x the budget.
  2. "Copy-paste between guides" is listed as an anti-pattern: "Duplicated content drifts apart; keep in one place, link from the other."
  3. "Upstream docs — Don't reproduce … tool docs — link to them" and "Already covered elsewhere" — much of the README content (linting, testing, environment setup) is already summarized in AGENTS.md or belongs in the existing docs.
  4. "Where Content Belongs" table says only project-wide invariants belong in AGENTS.md; detailed contributing docs like PR classification, code quality standards, and review process are better kept in the contributing guide and linked.

  If you want specific sections from the README added to AGENTS.md, I can help identify what's missing and add just those pieces while staying within the 200-line budget. Would you like to proceed that way instead?
```

### Test 2 - ensure user gets nudged towards domain-specific guides

```
❯ Add 2 paragraphs into our AGENTS.md describing the best practices for adding new CUDA kernels.

● Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

● Per the docs/contributing/editing-agent-instructions.md, CUDA kernel best practices are area-specific knowledge, which should go in a domain guide rather than directly in AGENTS.md (to keep it under its 200-line budget). The guide states:

  ▎ If it only matters for one area, put it in a domain guide.

  Would you like me to:
  1. Create a new domain guide (e.g., docs/contributing/cuda-kernels.md) and link it from the Domain-Specific Guides section of AGENTS.md — this is the recommended approach.
  2. Add it directly to AGENTS.md anyway, overriding the editing guidelines.

  Which do you prefer?
```
author: bbrowning

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces a comprehensive guide for editing agent instruction files, located at `docs/contributing/editing-agent-instructions.md`. This new document establishes crucial best practices, including token budget limits, criteria for adding content, and structural guidelines to ensure `AGENTS.md` remains lean and effective. `AGENTS.md` is updated to prominently link to this new guide, instructing agents to follow its rules. The guidelines are clear, well-structured, and aim to improve the long-term maintainability of agent instructions. The changes are of high quality and no issues were found during the review.

lgtm, worth a try - I think we'll be doing a lot of experimentation with this

The 200 line thing - https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory#write-effective-instructions

The domain-specific guides thing is aka "progressive disclosure" e.g. https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/best-practices#progressive-disclosure-patterns

commits.message:
[Docs] Add guide for editing agent instruction files

Establishes best practices for maintaining AGENTS.md and domain guides:
token budget limits, when NOT to add content, where content belongs,
what makes a good domain guide, anti-patterns to avoid, and a change
checklist.

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Signed-off-by: Ben Browning <bbrownin@redhat.com>
Merge branch 'main' into agents-md-editing-guide

title: [KVTransfer][Mooncake] Add heterogeneous TP support for disaggregated P/D in MooncakeConnector
body:
## Purpose ### Summary This PR adds heterogeneous tensor-parallel (TP) support to `MooncakeConnector` for disaggregated prefill/decode (P/D) in vLLM. Previously, the latest Mooncake integration still rejected heterogeneous TP paths with `NotImplementedError`. This change ports the heterogeneous TP transfer logic from an earlier Mooncake adaptation onto the latest vLLM Mooncake connector flow, while preserving the newer bootstrap-based worker registration and transfer coordination model. ### Motivation Mooncake is already integrated into the latest vLLM KV transfer stack, but heterogeneous TP is still blocked in the current implementation. This prevents Mooncake from being used in asymmetric P/D deployments where the prefill side and decode side use different TP sizes. This PR enables that scenario and brings Mooncake closer to feature parity with other heterogeneous-TP-capable KV transfer backends. ### What changed - Added heterogeneous TP-aware transfer planning for Mooncake KV transfers. - Added per-region / per-layer block length metadata to Mooncake transfer metadata. - Reused the latest Mooncake bootstrap server and `transfer_id`-based coordination flow. - Removed the previous `NotImplementedError` path for heterogeneous TP send/receive handling. - Added KV region expansion and offset calculation logic for asymmetric TP mappings. - Added validation for asymmetric KV region lengths between producer and consumer. - Set Mooncake to require `HND` KV cache layout for non-MLA models to make heterogeneous TP transfer safe. - Added lightweight unit tests covering: - required KV cache layout selection - heterogeneous TP sender transfer plan - multi-rank receive scheduling - guard behavior for invalid TP pairing ### Notes - The implementation keeps the latest Mooncake connector structure in place and only extends the missing heterogeneous TP logic. - MLA behavior remains unchanged. ## Test Plan Tested disaggregated prefill/decode with heterogeneous TP (Prefill TP=1, Decode TP=2) using Mooncake. This is just one of the results. In fact, we have tested various configurations on H20 GPUs, including the use_mla model and the non‑MLA model under different tensor parallel setups: P TP1 D TP2, P TP2 D TP1, and other cases. **1. Start Prefill Node (TP=1):** ```bash export MODEL="/mxstorage/pde_ai/models/llm/Qwen/Qwen3-8B/" export CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0 vllm serve $MODEL \ --port 7100 \ --gpu-memory-utilization 0.85 \ --served-model-name qwen \ --trust-remote-code \ --kv-transfer-config '{"kv_connector": "MooncakeConnector", "kv_role": "kv_producer"}' ``` **1. Start Decode Node (TP=2):** ```bash export MODEL="/mxstorage/pde_ai/models/llm/Qwen/Qwen3-8B/" export CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=2,3 vllm serve $MODEL \ --port 7200 \ --gpu-memory-utilization 0.85 \ --served-model-name qwen \ --trust-remote-code \ -tp 2 \ --kv-transfer-config '{"kv_connector": "MooncakeConnector", "kv_role": "kv_consumer"}' ``` ## Test Result ---
Essential Elements of an Effective PR Description Checklist - [x] The purpose of the PR, such as "Fix some issue (link existing issues this PR will resolve)". - [x] The test plan, such as providing test command. - [x] The test results, such as pasting the results comparison before and after, or e2e results - [ ] (Optional) The necessary documentation update, such as updating `supported_models.md` and `examples` for a new model. - [ ] (Optional) Release notes update. If your change is user facing, please update the release notes draft in the [Google Doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1YyVqrgX4gHTtrstbq8oWUImOyPCKSGnJ7xtTpmXzlRs/edit?tab=t.0).
author: JianDan0212

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces support for heterogeneous tensor parallelism in the Mooncake KV transfer connector, which is a significant feature enhancement for disaggregated prefill/decode scenarios. The changes are extensive, including new logic for transfer planning, KV region expansion, and validation for asymmetric TP configurations. The removal of `NotImplementedError` and the implementation of the transfer logic appear correct and well-structured. I've identified one potential issue where an assertion might be too restrictive for non-MLA models with varying layer configurations, which could limit future model support. Overall, this is a valuable contribution that brings Mooncake closer to feature parity with other KV transfer backends.

## Pull request overview This PR extends the vLLM KV transfer Mooncake connector to support heterogeneous tensor-parallel (TP) configurations for disaggregated prefill/decode by adding TP-ratio-aware transfer planning and per-region/per-layer block-length metadata needed to compute correct source/destination offsets. **Changes:** - Add heterogeneous-TP transfer planning helpers (region expansion, TP ratio planning, asymmetric length validation) and use them when building Mooncake transfer parameters. - Extend Mooncake transfer metadata to include per-region/per-layer block lengths and use it to validate and compute correct per-rank copy slices/offsets. - Remove the previous `NotImplementedError` gating heterogeneous TP on both send and receive paths, and add additional debug logging/sampling hooks. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

You can also share your feedback on Copilot code review. [Take the survey](https://www.surveymonkey.com/r/XP6L3XJ).

Thank you for your work. It looks good overall. I have provided some initial comments and will conduct a further review when I have time.In addition, this PR mainly includes two major parts: "support for heterogeneous TP" and "handling layers with different block lengths", which makes it less convenient to review :-/
I have a few minor questions, but overall LGTM.

Thanks for the PR @JianDan0212 and apologies for the delay in getting back to you.
I think the code is in a good state, only left a few comments.
Still I think it could really use a few clarifying comments here and there on the new functions you added.
I don't believe heteroTP is super intuitive to people that may want to contribute to the connector.
LGTM

title: [CPU][UX][Perf] Enable tcmalloc by default
body:
## Purpose

Enable tcmalloc by default for best OOB perf

To achieve this:
- at build time we search for libtcmalloc, and if we find it, we bundle it in the vLLM wheel under a libs directory
- at runtime we LD_PRELOAD the libtcmalloc under vllm_pkg/libs before spawning the Engine process
- this is only active for linux and (x86 or AArch64) - i.e. not Darwin and not PowerPC

This means that pre-built wheels will also have tcmalloc ON by default.

## Test Plan

I verified performance for llama 3.1 8b (bf16, w4a8, w8a8) using `vllm bench throughput` with:
- current state of vLLM & explicitly setting `LD_PRELOAD=/path/to/libtcmalloc_minimal.so.4` 
- this PR without setting any environment variables 

## Test Result

Performance is the same.

---
<details>
<summary> Essential Elements of an Effective PR Description Checklist </summary>

- [Y] The purpose of the PR, such as "Fix some issue (link existing issues this PR will resolve)".
- [Y] The test plan, such as providing test command.
- [Y] The test results, such as pasting the results comparison before and after, or e2e results
- [ ] (Optional) The necessary documentation update, such as updating `supported_models.md` and `examples` for a new model.
- [ ] (Optional) Release notes update. If your change is user facing, please update the release notes draft in the [Google Doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1YyVqrgX4gHTtrstbq8oWUImOyPCKSGnJ7xtTpmXzlRs/edit?tab=t.0).
</details>
author: fadara01

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request enables tcmalloc by default for CPU builds to improve performance. It adds logic to the build process to find and bundle the tcmalloc library into the wheel, and runtime logic to preload it. My review focuses on the robustness of this new logic. I've identified a couple of areas for improvement: using more specific exception handling when searching for tcmalloc, and ensuring robust version sorting when selecting the tcmalloc library at runtime.
Thanks! Verified it and worked well :)

[CPU][Perf] Enable tcmalloc by default

title: Better weight tying check for multimodal models
body:
For some models, `SomeVLTextConfig` may also have a `tie_word_embeddings` field. This is only the case if `SomeVLTextConfig` is also used for a text only version of the same model. For example:

```py
SomeVLModelForCausalLM:
    self.model = SomeLanguageModel(SomeVLTextConfig)
    self.lm_head = nn.Linear()
```

Therefore, the presence of `tie_word_embeddings` in `SomeVLTextConfig` cannot be used as a signal for whether `tie_word_embeddings` should be copied from `hf_config` to the `language_model` config.
author: hmellor

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request refines the logic within the `with_hf_config` method in `vllm/config/vllm.py` by simplifying the conditional check for `tie_word_embeddings` in multimodal models. The change removes a redundant check for the absence of `tie_word_embeddings` in the text configuration. Additionally, the accompanying comments have been significantly expanded to provide a more detailed and accurate explanation of how `tie_word_embeddings` should be handled across different multimodal model architectures, clarifying its relationship between the main model configuration and the language model's specific configuration. There is no feedback to provide from the review comments.

commits.message:

This pull request aims to fix a flaky test for Cohere/OpenAI embedding parity on ROCm by adding `ROCM_EXTRA_ARGS` to the test server's configuration. This introduces arguments to disable prefix caching and limit the maximum number of sequences to one on ROCm platforms. While this change successfully stabilizes the test, I have a concern that limiting sequences to one effectively disables batch processing, which undermines the purpose of the `test_batch_parity` test. My review includes a suggestion to handle this more explicitly to maintain test integrity.

title: [Bugfix] Pass drafter quant_config to ParallelLMHead in Eagle3
body:
Without this, quantized lm_head weights (e.g. INT8 per-channel) in Eagle3 drafter checkpoints fail to load because `ParallelLMHead` is created without a `QuantizationConfig` and doesn't expect `weight_packed` tensors.

## Test Plan
I have tested end2end inference with an eagle drafter for Qwen3-4B that I quantized locally.
The PR adds a unit test that failed before the change and now passes.

Note: Quantized `ParallelLMHead` is currently only supported by AWQMarlin, GPTQMarlin and cpu_wna16.
#37291 will enable it for compressed-tensors.
author: mgehre-amd

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request addresses a bug in Eagle3 models where quantized `lm_head` weights failed to load due to a missing `quant_config` during the initialization of `ParallelLMHead`. The fix involves passing the drafter's quantization configuration to `ParallelLMHead`. Additionally, a new unit test has been introduced to ensure that `ParallelLMHead` receives the correct `quant_config`, which validates the fix and helps prevent similar issues in the future.

commits.message:
[Bugfix] Pass drafter quant_config to ParallelLMHead in Eagle3

Without this, quantized lm_head weights (e.g. INT8 per-channel) in
Eagle3 drafter checkpoints fail to load because ParallelLMHead is
created without a QuantizationConfig and doesn't expect weight_packed
tensors.

Signed-off-by: Matthias Gehre <matthias.gehre@amd.com>

title: [CI] Fix realtime WebSocket timeout deadlock and unhandled model validation errors
body:
- Fix silent deadlock when ROCm JIT compilation exceeds the default 60s `VLLM_ENGINE_ITERATION_TIMEOUT_S`, causing `feed_tokens` in `voxtral_realtime.py` to die and hang generation forever
- Set `VLLM_ENGINE_ITERATION_TIMEOUT_S=600` in realtime test env overrides to match the existing 600s warmup receive timeout
- Fix `_check_model()` return value being ignored in `session.update` handler because model was marked validated even when not found
- Add missing `return` after `model_not_validated` error in `input_audio_buffer.commit` handler to prevent fall-through

## Test plan
- [x] `pytest tests/entrypoints/openai/test_realtime_validation.py -v -s`

cc @kenroche
author: AndreasKaratzas

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces several important fixes for the realtime WebSocket functionality. It addresses a potential deadlock on ROCm platforms by increasing the engine iteration timeout in the test environment. More critically, it corrects two error handling bugs: one where a model validation check's result was ignored, and another where a missing `return` statement caused fall-through logic after an error. My review includes a suggestion to improve the robustness of the `session.update` event handler to prevent potential `KeyError` exceptions from malformed client requests.
Thanks for the PR @AndreasKaratzas , could we add a small case to test for the two errors you added?

title: [ROCm][CI] Increase OpenAPI schema test timeouts
body:
The `test_openapi_stateless` schemathesis test is timing out on AMD CI nightly runs:
https://buildkite.com/vllm/amd-ci/builds/6877/steps/canvas?jid=019d2394-711a-4b3f-b211-dd66372f4c08&tab=output

Multiple endpoints hit the 10s default timeout, and even endpoints with the 60s timeout fail on ROCm due to infra sluggishness. In this PR, we apply a 3x timeout multiplier when running on ROCm for both `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` (10s to 30s) and `LONG_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` (60s to 180s) in `test_openai_schema.py`.

cc @kenroche
author: AndreasKaratzas

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces platform-specific timeouts for OpenAI schema tests, specifically increasing `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` and `LONG_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` by a factor of 3 when running on ROCm platforms. This change likely addresses test flakiness or failures due to longer execution times on ROCm. There are no review comments to provide feedback on.

commits.message:
[ROCm][CI] Increase OpenAPI schema test timeouts

Signed-off-by: Andreas Karatzas <akaratza@amd.com>

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request adds support for MLA models on Intel GPUs by enabling flash attention for prefill and Triton MLA for decode on the XPU platform. The changes involve updating XPU custom operations and adding platform-specific logic in the MLA attention and quantization layers. I've found a critical issue in the implementation of `forward_xpu` in the `QuantFP8` layer that could lead to runtime errors. My review includes a suggestion to fix this.

title: Flashinfer cuDNN backend for Qwen3 VL ViT attention
body:
## Purpose
Enable by `--mm-encoder-attn-backend=FLASHINFER`

### Details
- Add `FLASHINFER` as a backend for `MMEncoderAttention`
- Add computation for `cu_seqlens` and `sequence_lengths` before calling vision blocks. This is due to cuDNN backend's requirement to pass batch offsets and actual sequence lengths
- Pad `cu_seqlens`, `sequence_lengths` and `max_seqlen` to avoid cuDNN frontend graph recompilation
- Only support Qwen3 VL ViT, Qwen2.5 VL ViT is not supported

## Credit

NVIDIA's MLPerf Qwen3 VL MoE Submission Team @Anerudhan @b-mu @zhandaz @vedaanta @wangshangsam

## Test Plan
```
vllm bench mm-processor --mm-encoder-attn-backend=FLASHINFER --model Qwen/Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct

tests/kernels/attention/test_mha_attn.py:test_mha_attn_varlen_forward_flashinfer
```

## Test Result
```
vllm bench mm-processor --mm-encoder-attn-backend=FLASHINFER/FLASH_ATTN --num-prompt 1000 --model Qwen/Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct
```

#### mm request distribution:
1024 text tokens + 1 image (50% 256×256, 50% 720×1280), then generate 128 tokens.

#### Performance improvement (GB200):
***FLASH_ATTN***
```                                                                                                                                
MM Processor Metrics:
                     Stage  Mean Median   Std P99.0
          get_mm_hashes_ms  0.52   0.88  0.39  0.93
get_cache_missing_items_ms  0.01   0.01  0.00  0.02
     apply_hf_processor_ms 14.56  25.10 12.13 28.46
        merge_mm_kwargs_ms  0.05   0.05  0.02  0.10
   apply_prompt_updates_ms  1.71   2.67  1.07  2.93
     preprocessor_total_ms 16.85  28.79 13.60 32.21
        encoder_forward_ms  7.16   6.47  3.18 16.61
         num_encoder_calls  1.00   1.00  0.00  1.00

End-to-End Latency (ms):

Median   27018.73

```
***FLASHINFER***

get_cache_missing_items_ms  0.01   0.01  0.00  0.02

author: maxyanghu

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces support for the Flashinfer cuDNN backend for Qwen3 VL ViT attention, which is a valuable performance enhancement. The changes are well-structured, adding the necessary wrappers, constants, and logic to integrate the new backend. My main concern is the modification of `vllm/model_executor/models/qwen2_5_vl.py`. According to the pull request description, Qwen2.5 VL is not supported, yet the file is modified to include plumbing for the new backend. This introduces dead code and could lead to confusion. I've left a specific comment with a suggestion to remove these changes.

Thanks for the contribution and the idea makes sense to me!

I have a high-level suggestion - I think currently there are quite a bit of codes scattered in the `forward` for preparing metadata for flashinfer backend, could we encapsulate them in a separate method? I think this could also help us consolidate host-device sync and operate on torch as much as possible.

I'm thinking something like the following in the forward pass
```python
    cu_seqlens = np.repeat(grid_thw[:, 1] * grid_thw[:, 2], grid_thw[:, 0]).cumsum(
            axis=0, dtype=np.int32
        )
    cu_seqlens = np.concatenate([np.zeros(1, dtype=np.int32), cu_seqlens])

    # flashinfer path
    if self.attn_backend == AttentionBackendEnum.FLASHINFER:
        seq_lens_np = cu_seqlens[1:] - cu_seqlens[:-1]
        flashinfer_max_seqlen = self.bucket_flashinfer_max_seqlen(
            int(seq_lens_np.max()) if seq_lens_np.size > 0 else 0
        )

        cu_seqlens = torch.from_numpy(cu_seqlens).to(
            self.device, non_blocking=True
        )
        sequence_lengths = torch.from_numpy(seq_lens_np).to(
            self.device, non_blocking=True
        )
        sequence_lengths = self.add_padding_to_fi_seqlens(
            sequence_lengths, sequence_lengths.shape[0], 0
        )
        cu_seqlens = self.compute_flashinfer_cu_seqlens(
            cu_seqlens, rotary_pos_emb_cos, rotary_pos_emb_sin
        )

        max_seqlen = torch.tensor(flashinfer_max_seqlen, device=self.device)

    # Other
    else:
        cu_seqlens = torch.from_numpy(cu_seqlens_np)
        # sequence_lengths is still needed for the block signature
        seq_lens_np = cu_seqlens_np[1:] - cu_seqlens_np[:-1]
        sequence_lengths = torch.from_numpy(seq_lens_np).to(
            self.device, non_blocking=True
        )
        max_seqlen = self.compute_attn_mask_seqlen(cu_seqlens)
        cu_seqlens = cu_seqlens.to(self.device, non_blocking=True)

    hidden_states = hi
Let's get this in!

After https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/29752 enabled Efficient Video Sampling for `Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration` model, `Qwen3VLMoeForConditionalGeneration` model errors out due to newly added configuration `is_multimodal_pruning_enabled` not initialized and set.

author: maxyanghu

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request correctly resolves a `torch.dynamo` tracing issue by moving a non-traceable check for FlashInfer kernel availability from the forward pass to the `__init__` method of the `FusedMoE` class. The result of the check is cached in an instance attribute, which is then used in the `use_flashinfer_cutlass_kernels` property. This is a clean and effective fix that makes the code compatible with `torch.dynamo` without introducing any side effects.
LGTM

title: [Feature] Allow configuring FlashInfer workspace size
body:
Resubmission of https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/25344

Add `VLLM_FLASHINFER_WORKSPACE_BUFFER_SIZE` environment variable to configure FlashInfer workspace size
author: maxyanghu

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces a configurable workspace size for FlashInfer via the `VLLM_FLASHINFER_WORKSPACE_BUFFER_SIZE` environment variable. This is a valuable change as it centralizes configuration and removes hardcoded constants from multiple files, improving maintainability. The implementation correctly replaces the previous constants with the new environment variable. I have one suggestion to further improve maintainability by removing a magic number used for the default value.

### 💡 Codex Review Here are some automated review suggestions for this pull request.
ℹ️ About Codex in GitHub
Codex has been enabled to automatically review pull requests in this repo. Reviews are triggered when you - Open a pull request for review - Mark a draft as ready - Comment "@codex review". If Codex has suggestions, it will comment; otherwise it will react with 👍. When you [sign up for Codex through ChatGPT](https://openai.com/codex), Codex can also answer questions or update the PR, like "@codex address that feedback".

This looks good to me overall. I do think the best approach here is to determine this limit dynamically based on max batch size and whichever other parameters actually affect the required workspace buffer size. FlashInfer should probably implement this API and then expose it to vLLM. In the meantime, I welcome this patch.
LGTM, Thanks!

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request reverts the addition of PyTorch fallbacks for `fp8_mqa_logits` and `fp8_paged_mqa_logits`, making `deep_gemm` a hard requirement for this functionality. The changes correctly remove the fallback functions and conditional logic. However, the checks for `deep_gemm` availability have been changed from `is_deep_gemm_supported` to `has_deep_gemm`, which only verifies package installation and not hardware compatibility. To better align with the goal of failing clearly on unsupported hardware, I've suggested restoring the use of `is_deep_gemm_supported` and making the check a hard failure instead of a warning.

## Code Review

This pull request aims to fix an `IndexError` related to `prev_tool_call_arr` in `OpenAIToolParser`. The main fix involves clearing `prev_tool_call_arr` at the start of `extract_tool_calls`, which is a good defensive measure against state being carried over between requests. This PR adds support to selectively offload parameters to CPU based on name matching. One use case is to only offload experts weights for MoE weights, which is useful for low-concurrency settings. This is turned on by passing argument `--cpu-offload-params`

title: [Bugfix][ROCm][MoE] Fix mxfp4 oracle regressions from #37128
body:
Fixes several issues introduced by #37128 that broke gpt-oss on ROCm.

- Restore gfx950 gate for CK mxfp4 backend selection. The old code only picked CK on gfx950 via on_gfx950(), the refactor dropped this and let CK get selected on gfx942 where it crashes.
- Restore CK_MXFP4_MOE_DIM_ALIGNMENT (256) check. Models with intermediate_size not aligned to 256 (like gpt-oss-20b at 2880) hit a reshape error in aiter shuffle_scale_a16w4. Added is_supported_config to AiterExperts so the backend selector falls through to Triton.
- Restore hidden_pad/intermediate_pad for CK path. These were passed to rocm_aiter_ops.fused_moe() in the old code but got lost in the refactor. Added fields to FusedMoEQuantConfig and wired them through.

Tested on MI325X (gfx942):
- test_gpt_oss_speculative_reasoning_leakage passes
- GPQA eval via gpt_oss.evals: 56.76% (1584 questions, effort=low)
- Backend correctly falls back to Triton on non-gfx950

Related:
- https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/37811
- https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/37784
- https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/37786
- https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/37128

cc @kenroche

This pull request addresses several regressions related to MoE with mxfp4 quantization, primarily affecting ROCm platforms, which were introduced in a recent refactoring. The fixes include restoring platform-specific checks for the CK backend, correctly handling dimension padding, and resolving an issue with LoRA on NVIDIA. My review identifies a critical contradiction in one of the changes: while the PR description claims to enable mxfp4 LoRA on ROCm, the code continues to raise a `NotImplementedError`, albeit with a more descriptive message. This discrepancy needs to be resolved.

title: [Performance] Auto-enable prefetch on NFS with RAM guard
body:
## Purpose

Follow-up to #36012. Auto-enable checkpoint prefetching on NFS filesystems.

In #36012 prefetching was added as an opt-in `--safetensors-load-strategy=prefetch` flag. This PR makes it activate automatically when the model checkpoints reside on an NFS filesystem, which is a common setup in shared HPC/cloud clusters where model loading is often I/O-bound.

**How it works:**

1. **NFS detection** (`_is_nfs_path`): Parses `/proc/mounts` using the kernel's longest-prefix-match rule to determine whether a given path lives on an `nfs` or `nfs4` mount. Falls back to `False` on non-Linux systems or any read error.

2. **RAM guard**: Before enabling auto-prefetch on NFS, the total checkpoint size is compared against 90% of available RAM (`psutil.virtual_memory().available`). If checkpoints would exceed this threshold, prefetching is skipped and a warning is logged. This prevents the OS from evicting useful pages when the model is too large relative to available memory.

3. **Explicit opt-in unchanged**: `--safetensors-load-strategy=prefetch` remains unconditional — no RAM check is performed when the user explicitly requests prefetching.

## Test Result

Manual `vllm serve` runs on an NFS-backed storage without explicitly setting `--safetensors-load-strategy prefetch`. Checkpoint prefetching started automatically as expected.

---
<details>
<summary> Essential Elements of an Effective PR Description Checklist </summary>

- [x] The purpose of the PR, such as "Fix some issue (link existing issues this PR will resolve)".
- [x] The test plan, such as providing test command.
- [x] The test results, such as pasting the results comparison before and after, or e2e results
- [ ] (Optional) The necessary documentation update, such as updating `supported_models.md` and `examples` for a new model.
- [ ] (Optional) Release notes update. If your change is user facing, please update the release notes draft in the [Google Doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1YyVqrgX4gHTtrstbq8oWUImOyPCKSGnJ7xtTpmXzlRs/edit?tab=t.0).
</details>
author: arpera

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces an automatic prefetching mechanism for models on NFS filesystems, which is a great performance enhancement for distributed environments. The implementation includes a new function to detect NFS paths and logic to enable prefetching only when there is sufficient available RAM. The changes are well-structured. However, I found a bug in the NFS path detection logic where it would fail to identify paths on a root NFS mount. I've included a specific comment with a suggested fix for this issue. Once that is addressed, this PR will be a solid improvement.
Artem, could you also check what happens inside docker container? Do we see nfs from inside docker?
LGTM

commits.message:
[Performance] Auto-enable prefetch on NFS with RAM guard

Enable checkpoint prefetching automatically when the model
resides on an NFS filesystem, provided the total checkpoint
size does not exceed 90% of available RAM. Explicit
--safetensors-load-strategy=prefetch remains unconditional.

body:
nano_nemotron_vl: suppress readonly torch.from_numpy() warning in image and video resize paths.
No functional difference should be observed aside from the warning being suppressed.

[sanity_test.sh](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26187837/sanity_test.sh) - isolated tests with and without the diff, proving numerics are identical before and after.

* InfoVQA_VAL identical before and after.
* Video request identical before and after (greedy).

----

Full warning:
`nano_nemotron_vl.py:390: UserWarning: The given NumPy array is not writable, and PyTorch does not support non-writable tensors. This means writing to this tensor will result in undefined behavior. You may want to copy the array to protect its data or make it writable before converting it to a tensor. This type of warning will be suppressed for the rest of this program. (Triggered internally at /pytorch/torch/csrc/utils/tensor_numpy.cpp:213.)`
author: netanel-haber

reviews.body:

This pull request refactors image and video resizing paths to use a new helper function `_bicubic_from_ndarray` which also suppresses a PyTorch warning about non-writable NumPy arrays. The refactoring is applied correctly in `dynamic_preprocess` and `apply_params`. However, its use in `video_to_pixel_values` introduces a critical bug by ignoring a downsampling step. I've provided comments and suggestions to fix this regression.
LGTM

## Code Review

This pull request introduces an optimized GEMM kernel for the gpt-oss router, which demonstrates performance improvements for small batch sizes. The integration is well-supported by new unit tests and benchmarks. My review focuses on enhancing the robustness and error handling of the new CUDA kernel code. I've identified the use of `exit()` in a library context and `assert()` for error checking, which could lead to silent failures in release builds or abrupt process termination. I have suggested replacing these with PyTorch's standard error-checking mechanisms (`TORCH_CHECK`) and C++ exceptions to ensure proper error reporting.

title: Make microbatch optimization (DBO) work with general models
body:
## Purpose
Currently, microbatch optimization only works for text models, and the model must expose an internal `model` attribute (e.g. https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/5bf3c42d4c7e99f4898b18a715be515f44a22f44/vllm/model_executor/models/deepseek_v2.py#L1339-L1341)

Otherwise, vLLM fails to start. This PR extends microbatch optimization to general models, removing the requirement for text-only models and the need for an internal `model` attribute.

## Test Plan

vllm serve Qwen/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B --trust-remote-code --gpu-memory-utilization 0.8 -dp 2 --enable-expert-parallel --all2all-backend deepep_low_latency --enable-dbo

## Test Result

### Before the fix: 
For multimodal models, it gives the following error:
https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/34210

For models without an internal `model` attribute, it gives the following error:
```
(Worker pid=5836) (Worker_DP1_EP1 pid=5836) ERROR 03-23 20:25:23 [multiproc_executor.py:880]   File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/vllm/v1/worker/gpu_ubatch_wrapper.py", line 494, in __call__
(Worker pid=5836) (Worker_DP1_EP1 pid=5836) ERROR 03-23 20:25:23 [multiproc_executor.py:880]     return self._run_ubatches(ubatch_metadata, self.model)
(Worker pid=5836) (Worker_DP1_EP1 pid=5836) ERROR 03-23 20:25:23 [multiproc_executor.py:880]                                                ^^^^^^^^^^
(Worker pid=5836) (Worker_DP1_EP1 pid=5836) ERROR 03-23 20:25:23 [multiproc_executor.py:880]   File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/vllm/v1/worker/gpu_ubatch_wrapper.py", line 164, in __getattr__
(Worker pid=5836) (Worker_DP1_EP1 pid=5836) ERROR 03-23 20:25:23 [multiproc_executor.py:880]     raise AttributeError(
(Worker pid=5836) (Worker_DP1_EP1 pid=5836) ERROR 03-23 20:25:23 [multiproc_executor.py:880] AttributeError: Attribute model not exists in the runnable of cudagraph wrapper: Qwen3_5MoeForConditionalGeneration(
```

### After the fix

The server starts and runs successfully.

title: Fix Mamba state corruption from referencing stale block table entries (#37728) (#37728)
body:
Summary:
we saw zero-token-id response for a linear attention model. This diff fixes it.

Root cause is due to using stale mamba block, and this is triggered by DP dummy_run. It happens when one rank finishes a batch while other ranks are still running.  dummy_run(1) generates seq_len of [1,0,0,0..] to match other ranks' num_decode. The seq len 0 value indirectly maps to a stale mamba block gdn_attn.py. This padding is only needed for DP full cuda graph. That explains why TP or piecewise cuda graph works.

Padding up to cuda graph captured size itself works fine because num_reqs is the actual number of requests, and it correctly masks the block id of rest padded reqs properly in gpu_model_runner.py.

Also due to num_decodes = num_reqs (from attention/utils.py), the mask of block id beyond num_decodes here gdn_attn.py is no-op. Stale block id is within num_decodes requests anyway.

This diff cleans up block table when request is finished.


Test Plan: stress test with DP2 + prefix caching, verify no NaN/zero-token-id

Differential Revision: D97354760

Pulled By: minosfuture
author: minosfuture

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request addresses a critical issue of state corruption in Mamba models when using CUDA graphs with data parallelism. The root cause was identified as stale entries in the block table for finished requests. The fix introduces a `clear_row` method in the `BlockTable` and `MultiGroupBlockTable` classes to explicitly zero out block table entries on both CPU and GPU when a request is removed. This change is correctly integrated into the `InputBatch.remove_request` method, ensuring that slots for finished requests are properly cleaned up, thus preventing the use of stale data in subsequent operations, especially within a CUDA graph context. The implementation is sound and directly resolves the described problem.
LGTM! Can you fix DCO and pre-commit?

commits.message:
Fix Mamba state corruption from referencing stale block table entries (#37728) (#37728)

Summary:
we saw zero-token-id response for a linear attention model. This diff fixes it.

Root cause is due to using stale mamba block, and this is triggered by DP dummy_run. It happens when one rank finishes a batch while other ranks are still running.  dummy_run(1) generates seq_len of [1,0,0,0..] to match other ranks' num_decode. The seq len 0 value indirectly maps to a stale mamba block gdn_attn.py. This padding is only needed for DP full cuda graph. That explains why TP or piecewise cuda graph works.

Padding up to cuda graph captured size itself works fine because num_reqs is the actual number of requests, and it correctly masks the block id of rest padded reqs properly in gpu_model_runner.py.

Also due to num_decodes = num_reqs (from attention/utils.py), the mask of block id beyond num_decodes here gdn_attn.py is no-op. Stale block id is within num_decodes requests anyway.

This diff cleans up block table when request is finished.


Test Plan: stress test with DP2 + prefix caching, verify no NaN/zero-token-id

Differential Revision: D97354760

Pulled By: minosfuture

title: Add Ubuntu 24.04 support for Docker builds
body:
<!-- markdownlint-disable -->

## Purpose

  Adds Ubuntu 24.04 as an opt-in build target for vLLM Docker release images, closing #35118.

### Changes:
  - `docker/Dockerfile`: Add `UBUNTU_VERSION` ARG (default `22.04`) to parameterize
  `FINAL_BASE_IMAGE`. Install `python${PYTHON_VERSION}-dev` before apt cache cleanup (required by
  cmake 3.28 on Ubuntu 24.04 for `Development.SABIModule`). Remove `python3-pip` from final stage
  apt deps to avoid conflict with `get-pip.py` on Ubuntu 24.04 (pip 24.0 ships without a RECORD
  file). Remove `EXTERNALLY-MANAGED` marker to allow pip installs into the system Python.
  - `docker/docker-bake.hcl`: Add `test-ubuntu2404` and `openai-ubuntu2404` targets with
  `UBUNTU_VERSION=24.04`, `GDRCOPY_OS_VERSION=Ubuntu24_04`, and `FLASHINFER_AOT_COMPILE=true`.
  - `docker/versions.json`: Add `UBUNTU_VERSION` with default `"22.04"`.
  - `.buildkite/release-pipeline.yaml`: Add 4 new release pipeline steps (x86_64 + aarch64 for
  CUDA 12.9 and 13.0, all with Ubuntu 24.04) and 2 multi-arch manifest steps. CUDA 13.0 steps
  explicitly pass `BUILD_BASE_IMAGE=nvidia/cuda:13.0.1-devel-ubuntu24.04` since NVIDIA does not
  publish CUDA 13.x devel images for Ubuntu 20.04 (the Dockerfile default).

## Test Plan

  Built and tested all 4 Ubuntu 24.04 image variants locally:                                     
  ### CUDA 12.9 + Ubuntu 24.04                                                                      
  docker build --build-arg CUDA_VERSION=12.9.1 --build-arg UBUNTU_VERSION=24.04 \                 
    --build-arg GDRCOPY_OS_VERSION=Ubuntu24_04 --build-arg FLASHINFER_AOT_COMPILE=true \          
    --target vllm-openai -f docker/Dockerfile .                                                   
                                                                                                  
  ### CUDA 13.0 + Ubuntu 24.04                                                                      
  docker build --build-arg CUDA_VERSION=13.0.1 --build-arg UBUNTU_VERSION=24.04 \                 
    --build-arg GDRCOPY_OS_VERSION=Ubuntu24_04 --build-arg FLASHINFER_AOT_COMPILE=true \          
    --build-arg BUILD_BASE_IMAGE=nvidia/cuda:13.0.1-devel-ubuntu24.04 \                           
    --target vllm-openai -f docker/Dockerfile .    

## Test Result

 **Test:** `vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server` with `facebook/opt-125m`, prompt `"The capital of
   France is"`, `max_tokens=20`, `temperature=0`. Multi-GPU test uses `--tensor-parallel-size 4`
  across 4×A100. Ubuntu 22.04 rows verify no regression in existing builds.

  | Configuration | Single GPU | TP=4 |
  |---|---|---|
  | CUDA 12.9 + Ubuntu 24.04 *(new)* | Pass | Pass |
  | CUDA 13.0 + Ubuntu 24.04 *(new)* | Pass | Pass |
  | CUDA 12.9 + Ubuntu 22.04 *(existing)* | Pass | Pass |
  | CUDA 13.0 + Ubuntu 22.04 *(existing)* | Pass | Pass |

author: aasgaonkar

reviews.body:
## Code Review

The pull request successfully adds support for Ubuntu 24.04 across Docker builds, including new build arguments, parameterized base images, and updated release pipeline steps. The fix for the `EXTERNALLY-MANAGED` pip issue is crucial for compatibility with newer Ubuntu versions. However, there's an inconsistency in the `.buildkite/release-pipeline.yaml` regarding the `BUILD_BASE_IMAGE` for CUDA 13.0 Ubuntu 24.04 builds.
Looks reasonable to me!

commits.message:
Add Ubuntu 24.04 support for Docker builds

- Add UBUNTU_VERSION build arg to Dockerfile, defaulting to 22.04
- Parameterize FINAL_BASE_IMAGE to use UBUNTU_VERSION
- Fix pip EXTERNALLY-MANAGED issue for newer Ubuntu versions
- Add Ubuntu 24.04 build targets in docker-bake.hcl
- Add Ubuntu 24.04 release pipeline steps for x86_64 and aarch64
  (CUDA 12.9 and 13.0) with multi-arch manifests

Signed-off-by: aasgaonkar <aasgaonkar@nvidia.com>

Fix BUILD_BASE_IMAGE for CUDA 13.0 Ubuntu 24.04 builds

Update BUILD_BASE_IMAGE from ubuntu22.04 to ubuntu24.04 for the
CUDA 13.0 + Ubuntu 24.04 release pipeline steps to be consistent
with the UBUNTU_VERSION=24.04 build arg.

Signed-off-by: aasgaonkar <aasgaonkar@nvidia.com>

Fix Ubuntu 24.04 build failures in Docker

Three bug fixes found during local build testing of Ubuntu 24.04 images:

1. docker/Dockerfile (base stage): Install python${PYTHON_VERSION}-dev before
   apt cache cleanup. Ubuntu 24.04 ships cmake 3.28 which requires the
   Development.SABIModule component; without Python headers the csrc-build
   stage fails with "Could NOT find Python (missing: Python_INCLUDE_DIRS
   Development.SABIModule)". The install is best-effort (|| true) so it
   silently no-ops on Ubuntu 20.04/22.04 where the package is not in the
   default repos.

2. docker/Dockerfile (vllm-base stage): Remove python3-pip from apt deps.
   On Ubuntu 24.04, apt installs pip 24.0 without a RECORD file, causing
   get-pip.py to fail with "Cannot uninstall pip 24.0: no RECORD file was
   found". Removing python3-pip from apt lets get-pip.py install pip fresh
   with no conflict.

3. .buildkite/release-pipeline.yaml: Add FLASHINFER_AOT_COMPILE=true to
   the CUDA 13.0 + Ubuntu 24.04 build steps (x86_64 and aarch64). It was
   already set on the CUDA 12.9 + Ubuntu 24.04 steps; without it the
   CUDA 13.0 Ubuntu 24.04 images silently fall back to slow JIT compilation
   at runtime.

Signed-off-by: aasgaonkar <aasgaonkar@nvidia.com>

Fix missing build args in Ubuntu 24.04 CI and bake targets

- Add torch_cuda_arch_list='8.7 8.9 9.0 10.0+PTX 12.0' to x86_64 CUDA 12.9
  Ubuntu 24.04 release build, matching aarch64 and existing Ubuntu 22.04 builds
- Add torch_cuda_arch_list='8.7 8.9 9.0 10.0+PTX 12.0 12.1' to x86_64 CUDA 13.0
  Ubuntu 24.04 release build, matching aarch64 counterpart
- Add FLASHINFER_AOT_COMPILE=true to test-ubuntu2404 and openai-ubuntu2404
  docker-bake.hcl targets to match CI pipeline and avoid silent JIT fallback

Signed-off-by: aasgaonkar <aasgaonkar@nvidia.com>
Merge branch 'main' into ubuntu-2404-support
Merge branch 'main' into ubuntu-2404-support
Merge branch 'main' into ubuntu-2404-support

title: [Bugfix] Pass hf_token through config loading paths for gated model support
body:
## Purpose

Fixes #31894

The `hf_token` argument provided to the `LLM` instance was not forwarded to HuggingFace API calls during config loading, causing gated models to fail authentication when `hf_token` is passed explicitly (rather than via environment variable).

Three gaps existed:

1. `maybe_override_with_speculators()` called `PretrainedConfig.get_config_dict()` without the token, so speculators auto-detection failed for gated models.
2. `ModelConfig.__post_init__()` called `get_config()` without forwarding `self.hf_token`, so the main config loading path (`PretrainedConfig.get_config_dict`, `AutoConfig.from_pretrained`, `config_class.from_pretrained`) also lacked authentication.
3. `try_get_generation_config()` called `GenerationConfig.from_pretrained()` without the token, so generation config loading failed for gated models.

This fix threads `hf_token` through all three paths, using `token=` at the HuggingFace API boundary (matching the convention at [config.py L1031](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/ffb5b32b5bbf01e60b698e503f1cac59ce901162/vllm/transformers_utils/config.py#L1031)).

Note: PR #31974 attempted a similar fix but was closed with 0 commits.

## Test Plan

```bash
# Non-gated model (no regression)
python -c "from vllm.transformers_utils.config import maybe_override_with_speculators; \
  print(maybe_override_with_speculators('gpt2', None, False))"

# Gated model with explicit token (speculators path)
python -c "from vllm.transformers_utils.config import maybe_override_with_speculators; \
  print(maybe_override_with_speculators('meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf', None, False, hf_token='hf_...'))"
```

## Test Result

Non-gated models work as before. With `hf_token=None` (default), behavior is unchanged — HuggingFace Hub falls back to environment-based token lookup internally.

---

- [x] The purpose of the PR
- [x] The test plan
- [x] The test results
author: javierdejesusda

reviews.body:
## Code Review

The pull request introduces support for passing a HuggingFace token (`hf_token`) through the vLLM engine configuration. This token is now accepted as an argument in `create_engine_config` and `maybe_override_with_speculators`, and is subsequently used when calling `PretrainedConfig.get_config_dict` to enable authenticated access for fetching model configurations from HuggingFace.
Please merge from main to fix the pre-commit issue
LGTM, thanks for the work!

commits.message:
[Bugfix] Pass hf_token through config loading paths for gated model support

Fixes #31894

The hf_token argument provided to the LLM instance was not forwarded
to HuggingFace API calls during config loading, causing gated models
to fail authentication.

- Add hf_token parameter to maybe_override_with_speculators() and pass
  it as token= to PretrainedConfig.get_config_dict()
- Pass token=self.hf_token from ModelConfig to get_config() so it flows
  through to all HF config loading calls via **kwargs
- Add hf_token parameter to try_get_generation_config() and pass it to
  GenerationConfig.from_pretrained() and the get_config() fallback

Signed-off-by: javierdejesusda <javier.dejesusj9@gmail.com>
Merge remote-tracking branch 'origin/main' into fix/hf-token-speculators

title: [BugFix] fix VLLM_USE_STANDALONE_COMPILE=0
body: I broke this in one of the refactors, this fixes it and adds some testing
author: zou3519

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces a new test to validate the correctness of standalone compilation and implements a fix in `vllm/compilation/compiler_interface.py` to resolve a `FakeTensorMode` mismatch when `compile_fx` is used within Dynamo's tracing context. The review feedback highlights that the implemented fix relies on a private PyTorch API, `torch._guards._TLS.tracing_context`, which could lead to fragility and future breakage. It is recommended to add a comment explaining this dependency and to consider filing an issue with PyTorch to request a public API for this functionality.
Looks good to me. but is InductorAdapter still actively used by default? I didn't remember hitting this one in most testing

title: [BugFix] Fix order of compile logging
body:
Previously the logging went:
```
torch.compile took X s in total
Directly load AOT compilation from path ...
Initial profiling/warmup run took X s
```
This was a little weird because loading the AOT compilation is a part of "torch.compile".

This PR fixes it to
```
Directly load AOT compilation from path ...
torch.compile took X s in total
Initial profiling/warmup run took X s
```
author: zou3519

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request addresses a bug in the AOT compilation loading logic. The change correctly moves the counter increment for loaded AOT artifacts and the corresponding log message to be inside the conditional block that checks for the existence of the compiled artifact. This ensures that the metric and log are only updated when an artifact is actually loaded from the cache, fixing a bug where they would be updated even if the cache was missed. While the PR description focuses on logging order, the core of this change is a correctness fix for when the loading log and metric are recorded. The implementation is correct and improves the reliability of compilation metrics and logs.

"torch.compile".

This PR fixes it to

## Purpose
add nccl symmetric memory for all reduce. currently because of the limitations of torch compile we have to create a custom op and copy the input to a tensor allocated and registered with nccl.

author: Amir-19

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces NCCL symmetric memory for all-reduce operations to improve performance. It achieves this by adding a custom PyTorch operation that uses a pluggable allocator based on `ncclMemAlloc`. The changes are extensive, touching CUDA graph capturing, device communicators, and adding a new environment variable to control the feature. While the implementation is clever, I have identified a few critical issues. The feature has a hard dependency on a pre-release version of PyTorch, which is a significant risk. There is also a potential resource leak related to NCCL communication windows, and verbose logging is enabled by default during JIT compilation. These issues should be addressed to ensure the stability and usability of this new feature.

Thank you for making things work! I left some comments.
Some benchmarking comparing allreduce implementations would be helpful to figure out the input size bounds of the nccl symm mem allreduce.

Thank you for the update! 

1. We need to decide on allreduce algo dispatching. If we leave it for follow up PR, then let's keep constants in one place.
2. PR needs to have some tests: allreduce primitive, `get_nccl_mem_pool`, etc.
3. Compilation of nccl allocator needs to be improved for better UX in cases when there is no system NCCL installed.
4. PR needs a bit of cleaning (commented code, prints).

Thanks!

Overall, looks good to me.
Could you please add the test to .test-pipeline? Under label: Distributed Tests (B200)

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces support for the `ibm-granite/granite-4.0-1b-speech` model, updating documentation, test configurations to handle LORA paths conditionally, and the model registry. A critical issue was identified in `vllm/model_executor/models/granite_speech.py` where the `ISO639_1_SUPPORTED_LANGS` list was updated incorrectly. This change creates a superset of languages that are not universally supported across Granite Speech models (e.g., `granite-speech-3.3` does not support Japanese, and `granite-4.0` does not support Portuguese), potentially leading to runtime `KeyError`s. It is recommended to replace this static list with dynamic validation against the tokenizer's supported languages.
Thanks, LGTM

title: [Bugfix] Fix structured output crash on CPU due to pin_memory=True
body:
## Essential Checks

- [x] PR title follows the pattern `[Tag] Short description`
- [x] I have searched for related issues and checked existing PRs
- [x] I have run linting/formatting locally

## Purpose

Fix `RuntimeError: pin_memory=True requires a CUDA or other accelerator backend` crash when using structured output (guided decoding) on CPU-only deployments.

Fixes #37705

## Problem

`apply_grammar_bitmask()` in `vllm/v1/structured_output/utils.py` crashes on CPU when handling mixed batches (concurrent structured + non-structured requests):

1. **`pin_memory=True` is hardcoded** — `torch.tensor(out_indices, ..., pin_memory=True)` requires CUDA; fails on CPU-only systems.
2. **xgrammar CPU kernel expects `Sequence[int]`, not `torch.Tensor`** — `apply_token_bitmask_inplace_cpu()` only accepts a Python list for the `indices` argument.

Note: the existing CPU float32 workaround (added in #31901) was never reachable because the `pin_memory=True` crash occurs first.

## Fix

On CPU, pass `out_indices` as a plain Python list directly instead of converting to a pinned tensor. The GPU path with pinned memory is preserved.

## Test Plan

Tested by starting vLLM on CPU with `ibm-granite/granite-3.2-2b-instruct`, then sending concurrent plain + structured output (`response_format: json_schema`) requests. Without the fix, both requests return 500 and the EngineCore dies. With the fix, both succeed and the server stays healthy.

```python
import concurrent.futures
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="dummy")
MODEL = "ibm-granite/granite-3.2-2b-instruct"

def plain_request():
    return client.chat.completions.create(
        model=MODEL,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Tell me a story"}],
        max_tokens=200,
    )

def structured_request():
    return client.chat.completions.create(
        model=MODEL,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of France?"}],
        max_tokens=50,
        response_format={
            "type": "json_schema",
            "json_schema": {
                "name": "resp", "strict": True,
                "schema": {
                    "type": "object",
                    "properties": {"capital": {"type": "string"}},
                    "required": ["capital"],
                    "additionalProperties": False,
                },
            },
        },
    )

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as executor:
    f1 = executor.submit(plain_request)
    f2 = executor.submit(structured_request)
    print(f1.result())
    print(f2.result())
```
author: wjhrdy

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request effectively resolves a critical `RuntimeError` that occurred on CPU-only deployments due to `pin_memory=True` being hardcoded for `torch.tensor` creation. The changes correctly introduce conditional logic to handle CPU and GPU devices separately, ensuring that `pin_memory=True` is only applied when a CUDA device is available. Furthermore, it addresses the `xgrammar` CPU kernel's expectation of a Python list for indices by passing `out_indices` directly on CPU, which is a significant improvement for correctness and stability in mixed-batch scenarios. The updated type hint for `indices` also enhances code clarity.
Looks excellent -- do we need any validation on the testing side?
Seems reasonable to me, thanks!
thanks

commits.message:
[Bugfix] Fix structured output crash on CPU due to pin_memory=True

On CPU-only deployments, `apply_grammar_bitmask()` crashes with
`RuntimeError: pin_memory=True requires a CUDA or other accelerator
backend` when handling mixed batches of structured and non-structured
requests.

Two issues:
1. `pin_memory=True` is hardcoded in the `torch.tensor()` call for
   `out_indices` — this requires CUDA and fails on CPU.
2. The xgrammar CPU kernel (`apply_token_bitmask_inplace_cpu`)
   expects `Sequence[int]` for the `indices` argument, not a tensor.

Note: the existing CPU float32 workaround added in #31901 was never
reachable because the `pin_memory=True` crash occurs first.

Fix: on CPU, pass `out_indices` as a plain Python list. The GPU path
with pinned memory is preserved.

Fixes #37705

Signed-off-by: Willy Hardy <whardy@redhat.com>

title: docs: fix broken offline inference paths in documentation
body:
## Description
A manual audit of the documentation revealed that specific files in docs/models/pooling_models/ were using incorrect relative paths to reach the examples/ directory. Additionally, these files referenced a directory structure (examples/offline_inference/basic/) that did not match the actual repository layout.

## Changes
* docs/models/pooling_models/classify.md: Updated the relative path from ../../ to ../../../ to correctly reach the root from 3 levels deep. Corrected the directory sequence from offline_inference/basic/ to basic/offline_inference/.
* docs/models/pooling_models/embed.md: Applied the same depth and directory sequence corrections.

## Testing
* Manual Path Verification: Verified the new paths using ls from the documentation source directories:
ls docs/models/pooling_models/../../../examples/basic/offline_inference/classify.py
* Scope Refinement: Initially identified a wider range of files, but after manual verification, reverted changes to files (like docs/examples/README.md) where the original ../../ paths were already functionally correct.
author: vineetatiwari27

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request correctly updates numerous documentation files to reflect the new `examples/basic/offline_inference/` directory structure. The changes are consistent and address the broken links mentioned in the description. However, I found two instances where the path update seems to have been applied incorrectly, resulting in a duplicated `basic` directory in the path. These appear to be oversights from an automated search-and-replace and should be corrected to ensure all links are valid.
Thanks

title: [Bugfix] Force continuous usage stats when CLI override is enabled
body:
## Summary
- make `--enable-force-include-usage` return `(True, True)` from `should_include_usage()` so the server-level override always enables both final usage and continuous usage stats
- add regression coverage in `tests/entrypoints/test_utils.py` for forced usage behavior, including cases where request `stream_options` explicitly disable usage
- this is not duplicating existing work: I checked open PRs for `force include usage`, `continuous usage stats`, and `entrypoints utils usage stream_options`; the closest match is #24477, which adds token-details fields to continuous usage stats, while this PR changes the CLI override semantics

## Test Plan
- `python3 - <<'PY' ... PY` to compile `vllm/entrypoints/utils.py` and `tests/entrypoints/test_utils.py`, then execute isolated assertions for the new forced-override behavior
- attempted `pytest tests/entrypoints/test_utils.py -v -s`, but `pytest` is not installed in the current environment
- attempted `uv run pytest tests/entrypoints/test_utils.py -v -s`, but dependency resolution failed in this local environment because `uv` created a Python 3.13 env and hit the existing `smg-grpc-servicer[vllm]` self-dependency resolution issue

## Results
- isolated Python validation passed for the updated `should_include_usage()` behavior
- file compilation passed for both modified files

## AI Assistance
- this PR was prepared with AI assistance; the submitting human should review every changed line and run any additional relevant tests before merge
author: dsingal0

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request addresses a bug where the `--enable-force-include-usage` CLI flag failed to enable continuous usage statistics. The change in `vllm/entrypoints/utils.py` correctly modifies `should_include_usage()` to return `(True, True)` when this override is active, ensuring both final and continuous usage stats are enabled. The logic is now clearer due to an early return. Additionally, new regression tests in `tests/entrypoints/test_utils.py` have been added to verify the corrected behavior. The changes are sound and effectively resolve the issue.

commits.message:

Make --enable-force-include-usage override stream options so streaming responses always include usage and continuous usage stats when the server flag is enabled.

title: [XPU] Support Intel XPU hardware information collection in usage stats
body:
## Purpose
Currently, vLLM's usage stats reporting lacks specific hardware details when running on Intel XPU platforms, resulting in `gpu_type` and `gpu_count` being reported as `null`. 

This PR adds XPU platform detection in `vllm/usage/usage_lib.py` to correctly capture:
- `xpu_runtime` (torch.version.xpu)
- `gpu_count` (via torch.xpu)

title: [Core] add option to schedule requests based on full ISL
body:
## Purpose
The default behaviour of vLLM is to schedule requests if the first chunk of a request fits.

Observed problematic behavior (ISL numbers are mocked because this is customer data): When 4×100k ISL requests already occupy ~90% of the KV cache, the scheduler still admits a 5th 100k ISL request. It prefills in chunks until ~50k tokens are processed and KV cache hits ~100% capacity, triggering a preemption. The preempted request re-enters the queue and gets rescheduled, repeating the cycle.
In this particularly bad case, it creates a continuous prefill overhead that starves decode requests, dropping generation throughput from ~100 tok/s/GPU to as low as 1.5 tok/s/GPU.


This MR adds the option to calculate occupancy based on full ISL via env var. **We can also make this option the default, or available via CLI params**.
It is worth noting that TRT-LLM has an option that guarantees no evictions happen, based on full ISL + assuming max_new_tokens will be hit. I do not solve that, but instead just estimate full ISL.

## Test Plan


with the following command:
```
vllm serve Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507-FP8 \
	--host 0.0.0.0 \
	--port 8080 \
	--max-model-len 41001 \
	--tensor-parallel-size 8 \
	--data-parallel-size 1 \
	--enable-expert-parallel \
	--no-enable-prefix-caching \
	--api-server-count 1 \
	--enable-chunked-prefill &
sleep 10
VLLM_LOGGING_LEVEL=ERROR vllm bench serve \
	--backend vllm \
	--model Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507-FP8 \
	--dataset-name random \
	--random-input-len 40000 \
	--random-output-len 1000 \
	--num-prompts 100 \
	--ignore-eos \
	--ready-check-timeout-sec 600 \
	--port 8080 
```

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces a new environment variable to control the scheduler's behavior regarding full input sequence length (ISL) admission. The changes include adding the variable to the `envs.py` file, defining its default value, and integrating it into the scheduler logic to prevent over-admission of requests. The code changes look correct, and I have added a review comment to address a potential issue.
+1 that it would be nice to just have better defaults

commits.message:
[Scheduler] Add --scheduler-reserve-full-isl to prevent KV cache over-admission

With chunked prefill, the scheduler only checks if the current chunk
fits in KV cache when admitting a new request, not the full input
sequence. This leads to over-admission and KV cache thrashing: too many
requests get admitted based on small chunks, the cache fills up, and
requests get repeatedly preempted without making progress.

Add a scheduler config flag (--scheduler-reserve-full-isl) that gates a
pre-admission check verifying the full ISL fits in the KV cache before
admitting a new request. The check reuses the existing
coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate infrastructure so it correctly
handles sliding window, multiple KV cache groups, and prefix cache hits.

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Blanaru <48605845+DanBlanaru@users.noreply.github.com>
Made-with: Cursor

title: fix(kv-cache): increase hybrid attention grouping threshold from 1.25 to 1.5
body:
## Problem

Hybrid attention models combined with multi-layer speculative decoding drafters can exceed the 1.25 ratio threshold in `_get_kv_cache_groups_uniform_page_size`, causing excessive KV cache padding and crashes during initialization.

Example: [`amazon/GPT-OSS-20B-P-EAGLE`](https://huggingface.co/amazon/GPT-OSS-20B-P-EAGLE) is a 4-layer EAGLE drafter for `openai/gpt-oss-20b` (12 sliding-window + 13 full-attention layers). With the drafter, the full-attention count becomes 17, giving a ratio of 17/12 = 1.42 which exceeds 1.25.

## Fix

Increase the heuristic threshold from 1.25 to 1.5.

## Testing

Verified on `openai/gpt-oss-20b` + `amazon/GPT-OSS-20B-P-EAGLE` (4-layer drafter) on 8x B200. Without this fix, KV cache initialization fails. With this fix, all benchmarks (HumanEval, SPEED-Bench, MT-Bench) run successfully across C=1–128 at TP=1/2/4.
author: hai-meh-cs

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request addresses a crash during KV cache initialization for certain hybrid attention models by increasing a heuristic threshold from 1.25 to 1.5. The change is correct for the specified use case. However, my review includes a high-severity comment pointing out that using a hardcoded value is a fragile approach. I've recommended making this threshold configurable to prevent similar issues with future models and improve the long-term maintainability of the code.
LGTM
LGTM!
